supported_claims: One ton of burned McDowell/Pocahontas-seam coal releases ~2.61 metric tons CO2 (EPA AP-42 §1.1 factor applied to seam heat content); national-bituminous average is 2.32 t CO2/short ton.
The EPA 2023 social cost of carbon is $190/metric ton, anchored to Rennert et al. 2022 (Nature); the carbon/foreclosure term is $496 (2.61 x $190).
The Black Lung Benefits Program was established in 1969; the per-ton allocation against national tonnage since then is ~$1-1.50 (detailed Trust Fund walk carried in Ch2).
Reclamation bonds were required under the 1977 Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act.

The SCC has been a political object: Obama set it at $42/ton via EO 12866 global-scope methodology; Trump cut it to $1-7 by restricting geographic scope (flagged as ungrounded by Wagner, Anthoff, Cropper et al. 2021); Biden returned it to $51 (global scope); EPA 2023 raised it to $190.

The literary lineage of the 'mask'/survival-condition framing runs Dunbar (We Wear the Mask, 1896), Du Bois (Souls of Black Folk, 1903), Fanon (Black Skin White Masks, 1952), Ellison (Invisible Man, 1952).

The reparations-economics tradition (Coates, Darity, Mullen, Hamilton, Conley) supplies actor/coalition vocabulary; Public Choice (Buchanan, Tullock) supplies the extractor-reasoning/rent-seeking vocabulary.

The 2008 response architecture (TARP + Fed facilities) recapitalized bank balance sheets rather than restructuring household debt; Mian & Sufi's House of Debt (2014) analyzed the welfare cost of that choice vs the reachable alternative.

supported_claims: CS = RCV − B > 0 follows structurally from the Coase-extension (P1: future generations cannot bid — Coase 1960), the Pigou-extension (P2: B prices only the externality tail, B < RCV — Pigou 1920), and empirical inadequacy (P3) — Theorem §10.1a/§10.1b.

RCV − Hotelling rent = CS: the framework extends Hotelling 1931's scarcity-rent model to price the intergenerational foreclosure cost Hotelling's private-owner optimization structurally excludes (§4, §14.1).

Hartwick's Rule (Hartwick 1977) makes CS = 0 only under the strong-substitutability assumption S_max = 1; RCV generalizes to S_max ≤ 1, so no investment policy fully compensates for foreclosure when S_max < 1 (§14.2, §14.6).
The framework adopts Weitzman 2001 declining discount rates, biasing RCV estimates downward (conservative-by-construction / META-CLAIM 3) (§14.3).

Ostrom 1990's eight design principles govern renewable-commons coordination but are necessary-but-insufficient for the non-renewable extraction case and presume substantially-aligned stakeholders (heterogeneous-stakeholder scope boundary) (§6.6, §14.5, l.604).
The carbon damage term uses the US EPA Social Cost of Carbon central estimate of $190/ton CO₂ at 2% discount rate (Rennert et al. 2022) (§3.3, §11.1, §16.4).

The McDowell CO₂ emission factor of 2.61 mt CO₂/short ton (Pocahontas-seam basis) and 2.32 mt CO₂/short ton (national-bituminous-average basis) both derive from EPA AP-42 §1.1, with McDowell heat content from USGS Professional Paper 1625-C + WV Geological Survey (§7.4, §11.6).

Norway's GPFG (~$2.0T end-2025, 21,268 bn NOK; ~70–80% state capture rate; ~5.62M population) operationalizes Hartwick's Rule and is the canonical existing B₂ exemplar — sourced to NBIM Annual Report 2025, Statistics Norway 2025, Norwegian Petroleum Directorate (§11.5).

The bond apparatus (B₁ Restitution / B₂ Foreclosure) is a principled extension of existing US regulatory practice — SMCRA 1977, CERCLA/RCRA financial assurance, BLM 43 CFR Part 3162, BSEE 30 CFR Part 250+254, ~36 state plugging/abandonment regimes, Environmental Impact Bonds (Balboa 2016) (§5).
The framework is the Rawlsian veil (Rawls 1971) applied to resource extraction, implemented via the stock-dependent utility term U(R,t,Q(t)) (§14.4).
Harvey 2003's accumulation-by-dispossession names the mechanism; RCV measures its magnitude (Harvey diagnoses, RCV quantifies) (§14.7).
The logarithmic scarcity-amplification form follows welfare-economics precedent (Atkinson 1970 + Cobb & Douglas 1928) (§1).
CS > 0 holds across the entire plausible discount-rate range from Nordhaus (5.5%) to Stern (1.4%); only the magnitude (IPG) varies — confirmed by the §16.4 sensitivity table (§14.3, §16.4).
